definition: A person or business that might become a customer. A lead can come from a form, funnel, call, ad, referral, website chat, social media message, import, or manual entry.
example: A homeowner fills out a seller valuation form. That person is now a real estate lead.

definition: The person record inside the CRM. It usually stores name, phone, email, tags, notes, tasks, custom fields, conversations, and appointment history.
example: Maria Santos is a contact with the tag Dental Implant Interest.

definition: Customer Relationship Management. In plain English, it is the organized database where a business tracks leads, customers, conversations, tasks, and sales activity.
example: A clinic uses the CRM to see who requested a consultation and who still needs follow-up.

definition: A label added to a contact for organization, segmentation, or automation. Tags should have a clear purpose.
example: New Lead, No Show, VIP Customer, and Whitening Interest are tags.

definition: A field created to store specific information that the business needs. Use fields for values, not broad labels.
example: Pet Type, Preferred Service, Budget, Property Address, and Insurance Provider are custom fields.

definition: A saved filtered view of contacts. It helps teams quickly find a useful group of people.
example: A smart list can show all new leads from the last seven days who have not booked.

definition: A set of fields that collects information from a lead. Keep forms short unless the business truly needs more details.
example: A grooming form asks for name, phone, pet type, service, and preferred appointment day.

definition: One question or input inside a form or contact record.
example: Phone Number is one field. Service Interest is another field.

definition: A focused path that guides a visitor toward one clear action, usually from landing page to form to thank-you page.
example: A dental implant funnel explains the offer, collects lead details, and invites the lead to book.

definition: The page where a visitor arrives after clicking an ad, post, email, or link. It should focus on one offer and one action.
example: A free buyer guide landing page asks visitors to enter their email.

definition: The page shown after a form is submitted. It confirms what happened and tells the lead the next step.
example: After submitting a clinic form, the thank-you page says to book a consultation.

definition: A phone inquiry from a possible customer. Calls should be tracked because missed calls can still become leads.
example: A missed call after business hours can trigger an automatic text-back.

definition: A paid promotion on platforms like Facebook, Google, Instagram, or TikTok that tries to attract attention and generate leads.
example: A dental clinic runs a Facebook ad for free implant consultations.

definition: A lead recommended by an existing customer, partner, friend, or another business.
example: A current grooming customer recommends a neighbor with a new dog.

definition: A visual tracker that shows where each deal or opportunity is in the sales process.
example: New Lead, Contacted, Booked, Won, and Lost are pipeline stages.

definition: The deal record connected to a contact. It tracks where the lead is in a pipeline.
example: Maria is the contact. Her implant consultation deal is the opportunity.

definition: The booking system that controls availability, appointment length, buffers, team assignment, and booking links.
example: A grooming shop calendar allows 60-minute appointments with a 15-minute buffer.

definition: A rule-based automation that starts from a trigger and runs actions.
example: When a form is submitted, send an SMS, create an opportunity, and notify staff.

definition: The event that starts a workflow.
example: Form Submitted, Tag Added, Appointment Booked, Customer Replied, and Missed Call are triggers.

definition: Something a workflow does after it starts.
example: Send SMS, Add Tag, Create Task, Move Opportunity, and Notify User are actions.

definition: A delay before the next workflow action.
example: Wait one day, then check if the lead booked an appointment.

definition: A rule that checks whether something is true before deciding what happens next.
example: If the contact has an appointment, stop the nurture messages.

definition: A URL that receives event data from another app. It is often used to send GHL data to Make, n8n, Zapier, or custom apps.
example: A GHL form submission sends lead details to a Make webhook.

definition: A connection between two or more tools so data can move between them.
example: GHL can integrate with Google Sheets through Make, n8n, or Zapier.

definition: A structured way for software systems to talk to each other. Beginners should understand the concept before trying to code with it.
example: An API can let one tool create or update records in another tool.

definition: A visual automation platform useful for multi-step scenarios, data formatting, routers, and app connections.
example: Make can receive a GHL webhook, add a row to Google Sheets, and send a Slack message.

definition: A flexible automation platform often used by technical builders, including self-hosted workflows and custom logic.
example: n8n can receive a webhook, enrich lead data, and update another system.

definition: A beginner-friendly automation platform for connecting apps with simple triggers and actions.
example: Zapier can send a new GHL lead to a spreadsheet or email tool.

definition: A private credential such as an API key, token, password, or private webhook URL.
example: Never paste an API key into a public page, screenshot, or browser code.

definition: A reusable package of GHL assets, such as workflows, funnels, fields, calendars, and pipelines, that can help set up new client accounts faster.
example: An agency can create a dental clinic snapshot and adapt it for each new clinic client.

definition: A separate client workspace inside an agency HighLevel account.
example: A marketing agency may have one sub-account for each local business client.

definition: A human-written detail saved on a contact record so the team remembers useful context.
example: A note can say: Prefers afternoon calls and asked about financing.

definition: A reminder or assignment for a team member to do something for a contact.
example: Create a task for the front desk to call a hot lead today.

definition: One step inside a pipeline that shows where a lead or deal is in the sales process.
example: New Lead, Contacted, Booked, Won, and Lost are stages.

definition: A simple label that describes the general condition of a lead.
example: A lead can be new, active, won, lost, or unqualified.

definition: When a person moves an opportunity to another pipeline stage after checking what happened.
example: An agent manually moves a buyer lead to Offer Made after the client submits an offer.

definition: When a workflow moves an opportunity after a clear event happens.
example: A workflow moves a lead to Booked after an appointment is scheduled.

definition: The steps a business follows to turn a lead into a customer.
example: Inquiry, follow-up, appointment, proposal, won, and lost can be a sales process.

definition: A scheduled meeting, service, consultation, or call between the business and the lead or customer.
example: A dental consultation booked for Friday at 10 AM is an appointment.

definition: The days and times when a staff member or business can accept bookings.
example: A clinic may accept consultations Monday to Friday from 9 AM to 4 PM.

definition: Extra time before or after appointments so staff are not overloaded.
example: A 15-minute buffer after grooming gives staff cleanup time.

definition: A link a lead can use to choose an available appointment time.
example: A clinic sends a booking link after a lead submits a consultation form.

definition: A message sent before an appointment so the lead remembers to attend.
example: Send reminders 24 hours and 2 hours before the appointment.

definition: When someone books an appointment but does not attend.
example: Reminder texts help reduce no-shows.

definition: A longer form that asks more qualifying questions.
example: A real estate survey can ask whether the lead is buying, selling, or investing.

definition: Something useful offered in exchange for contact information.
example: A buyer guide, checklist, coupon, or free consultation can be a lead magnet.

definition: The place where the team manages messages and replies from leads and customers.
example: Staff can answer SMS and email replies from the Conversations inbox.

definition: A text message sent to a lead or customer.
example: Send an SMS after a form submission to invite the lead to book.

definition: A reusable email message that can be sent manually or by automation.
example: A clinic can use a consultation reminder email template.

definition: A message or action used to continue the conversation after a lead shows interest.
example: A follow-up text asks if the lead wants help booking.

definition: The process for what happens when a lead replies to an automated message.
example: When a lead replies, stop the nurture workflow and notify staff.

definition: A sequence of helpful follow-up messages sent over time to move a lead toward action.
example: A seven-day dental nurture sequence can explain benefits and invite booking.

definition: A way for a person to stop receiving marketing messages.
example: SMS campaigns should respect opt-out requests.

definition: A workflow split where one path runs if a condition is true and another path runs if it is false.
example: If the lead booked, stop messages. Else, send a reminder.

definition: A rule that stops or removes a contact from a workflow when the workflow is no longer appropriate.
example: Stop the nurture workflow when the lead books an appointment.

definition: The information you collect from a client before building, including goals, tools, process, roles, and rules.
example: Ask who follows up, what counts as a hot lead, and what messages should be sent.

definition: A simple outline of what happens from lead source to final outcome.
example: Ad click, form submission, SMS, booking, reminder, consultation.

definition: A list used to confirm forms, tags, workflows, reminders, notifications, and pipeline updates work before launch.
example: Submit a fake lead and confirm every expected action happens.

definition: Quality assurance before a system goes live for real users.
example: Check links, phone numbers, messages, timezone, and stop rules before launch.

definition: Finding why something failed and fixing the cause.
example: Check workflow history when a reminder SMS did not send.

definition: Fake test information used to check a system without exposing real client or customer data.
example: Use Test Lead with a safe test phone number when checking a workflow.

definition: The final transfer of a completed setup to the client or team, including instructions and notes.
example: Give the client a launch checklist and explain how to manage new leads.

definition: A practical sample project that shows you can solve a client-style problem.
example: A missed-call text-back and review request system can be a portfolio project.

definition: A short explanation of the client problem, your solution, tools used, workflow summary, and result.
example: A case study can explain how a grooming shop reduced missed bookings.

definition: A short achievement statement that describes practical work you can do.
example: Built mock GHL appointment workflow with reminders, pipeline updates, and staff notifications.

definition: Practice answering questions about your skills, process, decisions, and project work.
example: Explain how you would test a lead nurture workflow before launch.

definition: The agreed boundary of what a project includes and does not include.
example: This project includes one funnel and one workflow, but not paid ad management.

definition: A concrete item you promise to produce for a project.
example: A booking calendar, reminder workflow, and testing checklist are deliverables.

definition: A completion document from this training app that is not official HighLevel certification.
example: Use it as proof of training completion, not as an official HighLevel credential.
